summary: Gruber dissects Tim Sweeney's call for a 'universal app store' that works across all platforms, exposing the contradiction in proposing a single store as the solution to an alleged duopoly. He notes that Sweeney has now confirmed what Gruber long predicted: Epic's fight against Apple and Google's app stores was always going to extend to game console platforms too, despite Sweeney's earlier hand-wavy excuses for why consoles were different. Gruber frames this as Sweeney having a tantrum after Epic lost its lawsuit against Apple, dropping the pretense of principled opposition. A lengthy footnote critiques Bloomberg's continued refusal to retract its discredited Big Hack story while acknowledging their importance as a news organization.
key_points: Sweeney's proposal for a single universal store contradicts his own complaint about duopoly — replacing two stores with one is consolidation, not competition
Sweeney has now openly targeted game console stores (Xbox, PlayStation, Switch) after previously giving them a pass with flimsy justifications about hardware profitability
Epic's loss in the Apple lawsuit appears to have prompted Sweeney to abandon his more careful, strategic messaging
Gruber maintains his ongoing critique of Bloomberg's journalistic credibility while acknowledging their essential role in tech reporting
money_quotes: The solution to an ostensibly problematic duopoly is ... a single universal store? And we're supposed to take this without laughing?
And, gee, I wonder which company Tim Sweeney thinks should own and run this store?
That reeked of bullshit from the get-go. Now he's made it clear.
Epic got their clocks cleaned in their lawsuit against Apple, and now Sweeney's having a tantrum and letting it all hang out.
If I were on the PlayStation, Xbox, or Nintendo Switch store teams, I wouldn't trust Epic as far as I could throw them.
Bloomberg, of course, remains the outfit that shit its journalistic pants with The Big Hack.
I won't let go of this Big Hack fiasco because Bloomberg is too good an institution to leave such an egregious and high-profile mistake uncorrected.
key_insight: Sweeney's call for a universal app store reveals that Epic's fight was never about open competition but about positioning Epic as the gatekeeper, and losing the Apple lawsuit stripped away the strategic pretense.
